text:
*[Bug 19012] foreign::write.foreign() generates incorrect SAS INPUT statement for character variables*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19012>

Tom Abernathy (<mailto:tom.abernathy@gmail.com|tom.abernathy@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |tom.abernathy@gmail.com

--- Comment #7 from Tom Abernathy (<mailto:tom.abernathy@gmail.com|tom.abernathy@gmail.com>) ---
As already noted the lack of a $ after variable a (or the presence of a $ after
the variable c) in the generated INPUT statement has no impact on the execution
of the data step in SAS.

But looking at this R function there are a number of Bug/Issues with the way it
now works.

- Includes only the character variables in the LENGTH statement 
  - This changes the order that the variables will appear in the dataset
- Does not make unique variable names (SAS names are case insensitive)
- Does not generate valid string literals when value has embedded quotes
  - In SAS code embedded quotes must be doubled
- Uses double quotes instead of single quotes for string literals
  - The SAS macro processor will evaluate strings made using double quotes
- Does not account for multibyte characters when calculating lengths
- Does not check if a character variable's length exceeds SAS limit (32K bytes)
- Does not support VALIDVARNAME=ANY
- Does not support VALIDFMTNAME=LONG
- Does not support VALIDMEMNAME=EXTEND 
- Does not prevent using SAS reserved names in data step
- Uses too small a width on DATETIME format so that century will not print
- Does not include TRUNCOVER option on the INFILE statement
- Does not transfer fractional seconds for Datetime (POSIX) values
- Supports the "dates" class from the chron package but not the "times" class
- Does not detect variable types that write.table does not support ("list" for
example)

I have working code that fixes most of these now, but I cannot find much
existing tests for this function.  I am happy to work on making tests also, but
would appreciate guidance on best way to structure the tests.

- Includes only the character variables in the LENGTH statement 
  - This changes the order that the variables will appear in the dataset
- Does not make unique variable names (SAS names are case insensitive)
- Does not generate valid string literals when value has embedded quotes
  - In SAS code embedded quotes must be doubled
- Uses double quotes instead of single quotes for string literals
  - The SAS macro processor will evaluate strings made using double quotes
- Does not account for multibyte characters when calculating lengths
- Does not check if a character variable's length exceeds SAS limit (32K bytes)
- Does not support VALIDVARNAME=ANY
- Does not support VALIDFMTNAME=LONG
- Does not support VALIDMEMNAME=EXTEND 
- Does not prevent using SAS reserved names in data step
- Uses too small a width on DATETIME format so that century will not print

- Does not transfer fractional seconds for Datetime (POSIX) values

text:
*[Bug 19016] as.numeric("NA") throws an unnecessary coercion warning*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19016>

Benjamin Tyner (<mailto:btyner@gmail.com|btyner@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |btyner@gmail.com

--- Comment #1 from Benjamin Tyner (<mailto:btyner@gmail.com|btyner@gmail.com>) ---
(In reply to Dan Chaltiel from comment #0)
The existing behavior is as documented, and dictated by the ISO C and POSIX
standards for atof,

   <https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/string/byte/atof>

Specifically, the C99 standard and later added requirements for:

   INF / INFINITY: (case-insensitive) Must be parsed as the numeric infinity.

   NAN: (case-insensitive) Must be parsed as the numeric not-a-number.
